coredata.dc:title: Highly luminescent N, S- Co-doped carbon dots and their direct use as mercury(II) sensor
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yu
Kim, Sung-Hoon
Feng, Liang
coredata.dc:description: Heteroatom doping has been proven as an efficient way to improve the fluorescent efficiency of carbon dots. Co-doping with heteroatoms may introduce more active sites to carbon dots, which would broaden applications of CDs in sensing. In this work, highly luminescent nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (NSCDs) were synthesized through a facile one-step microwave assisted method by using citric acid and rubeanic acid as carbon, nitrogen, and sulfur sources. The well-isolated NSCDs not only exhibit an enhanced fluorescent efficiency with a relatively high quantum yield up to 17.6%, but also show potential use as a multi-sensing platform based on their fluorescence “on-off-on” and color changing behaviors. The NSCDs can be directly used for the selective determination of mercury cations without any functionalization. The detection limit is approximately calculated as 0.18 μM and linear range is 0–20 μM. The sensing mechanism is proposed as coordination reaction induced by oligomers upon the carbon core. Furthermore, in the presence of cyanide anions, the fluorescence shows linear recovery associated with the concentration of cyanide, indicating its potential usage for the detection of cyanide ion. The optimized pH range for such fluorescence “on-off-on” sensing system is investigated as pH 6–8, suggesting potential applications in bio-sensing and imaging area. In addition, by adding hydrosulfide anion to NSCDs@Hg2+ complex, a notable color change could be clearly observed due to the formation of fuscous HgS. In application, a handy test paper for direct and rapid detection of Hg2+ is manufactured for the evaluation of usage of NSCDs in the real circumstance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Multi-sensing
Mercury
Cyanide
Hydrosulfide

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Calculation of relative FLQY of NSCDs
Manufacture of NSCDs-based test paper and its application in real sample detection
Multi-sensing for Hg2+, CN− and HS− ions in aqueous condition
Rapid detection of Hg2+ in real water

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Excitation-dependent FL spectra and (B) Emλmax of the NSCDs (from 320 to 400 nm); (C) UV–vis absorption, excitation and emission spectra of NSCDs in aqueous solution (15 μg/mL), λex = 360 nm and λem = 448.

(A) Emission spectra upon the addition of various metal ions to 7.4 pH buffer solution of NSCDs; (B) Degree of FL quenching at 448 nm before (FL0) and after (FLi) adding clearly demonstrates the selectivity of NSCDs toward metal ions; (C) FL spectra of 15 μg/mL NSCDs upon the addition of different concentration of Hg2+; (D) the photos of 15 μg/mL NSCDs solutions in the absence (left) and presence (right) of mercury(II) ions illuminated by UV light of 365 nm.

(A) Emission spectra upon the addition of various anions to 7.4 pH buffer solution of CD-Hg; (B) FL spectra of CD-Hg (15 μg/mL NSCDs with 20 μM Hg) upon the addition of different concentration of CN− in BR buffer solution (pH 7.4).

(A) Absorption spectra of CD-Hg (15 μg/mL NSCDs with 20 μM Hg) upon the addition of different anions in pH 7.4 BR buffer solution; (B) Absorbance changes at 360 nm before (AbsCD-Hg) and after (Absi) adding anions; (C) Integration of absorption and emission spectra for anions detection based on CD-Hg platform.
Changes of FL intensities of 15 μg/mL NSCDs at 448 nm in “on-off-on” process at different pH value.
NSCDs-based test papers and original filter paper under daylight and UV lamp.
N, S-doped carbon dots and their properties.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: File the highest-FLQY-possessed sample among experiments.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: File the highest-FLQY-possessed component after HPLC processing.

head.ce:title: Highly luminescent N, S- Co-doped carbon dots and their direct use as mercury(II) sensor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Lab of Separation Science for Analytical Chemistry, Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Dalian 116023, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Lab of Separation Science for Analytical Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Advanced Organic Materials Science and Engineering, Kyungpook National University, Daegu, 702-701, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Advanced Organic Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heteroatom doping has been proven as an efficient way to improve the fluorescent efficiency of carbon dots. Co-doping with heteroatoms may introduce more active sites to carbon dots, which would broaden applications of CDs in sensing. In this work, highly luminescent nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (NSCDs) were synthesized through a facile one-step microwave assisted method by using citric acid and rubeanic acid as carbon, nitrogen, and sulfur sources. The well-isolated NSCDs not only exhibit an enhanced fluorescent efficiency with a relatively high quantum yield up to 17.6%, but also show potential use as a multi-sensing platform based on their fluorescence “on-off-on” and color changing behaviors. The NSCDs can be directly used for the selective determination of mercury cations without any functionalization. The detection limit is approximately calculated as 0.18 μM and linear range is 0–20 μM. The sensing mechanism is proposed as coordination reaction induced by oligomers upon the carbon core. Furthermore, in the presence of cyanide anions, the fluorescence shows linear recovery associated with the concentration of cyanide, indicating its potential usage for the detection of cyanide ion. The optimized pH range for such fluorescence “on-off-on” sensing system is investigated as pH 6–8, suggesting potential applications in bio-sensing and imaging area. In addition, by adding hydrosulfide anion to NSCDs@Hg2+ complex, a notable color change could be clearly observed due to the formation of fuscous HgS. In application, a handy test paper for direct and rapid detection of Hg2+ is manufactured for the evaluation of usage of NSCDs in the real circumstance.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), a promising family of nanocarbons consisted of quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles with sizes below 10 nm, have recently attracted tremendous attentions due to their advantageous properties [1–4]. Compared with conventional semiconductor quantum dots, CDs are superior due to their facile manufacture, splendid water solubility, low toxicity, preferable photo-/chemical stability, and excellent biocompatibility [5–7]. Numerous studies have devoted to extend the payload of the functional scaffolds of CDs ranging from sensing, imaging, drug delivery to optoelectronic devices [8–19]. Since the first observation accidentally found during purification of single-walled carbon nanotube through an arc-discharged method in 2004 [20], works on the design of versatile chemo- and bio-probes based on CDs have never been stopped. In most synthetic methods, the surface of CDs is enveloped by the hydrophilic groups –OH, –CO or –COOH [21,22]. This unique property provides the possibility of surface functionalization, which makes CDs specifically sensitive to various metal cations either in a form of fluorescent quenching (FL “on-off”) or enhancement [23–26].

These CDs, however, suffer from poor luminescence efficiency and low sensitivity, thus largely limiting their applications. Doping CDs with heteroatoms has been proven as an efficient way to improve the fluorescent efficiency and sensing selectivity of carbon dots. In recent years, nitrogen doping for CDs has been reported to strongly enhance fluorescent quantum yields (FLQY) of CDs and found for some sensing applications via the versatile coordinating behavior of terminal amine groups [27–29]. Technically, co-doping CDs with more heteroatoms will introduce more active sites, leading unique sensing performance. Therefore, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs have been investigated with some peculiar properties [30–32]. Their usage as fluorescent probes for rapid detection, has been highly expected.

Citric acid (Duksan) and dithiooxamide (rubeanic acid, Sigma–Aldrich) were used to synthesize NSCDs. All analytical reagents for ion titration (tetrabutylammonium salt of anions, perchlorate salt and acetate salt for cations) were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich and used as received. The rest of chemicals were commercially available with high grade and were used without further puriﬁcation. Distilled water was used for the dilution throughout the experiment. Britton–Robinson (BR) buffer solution (pH range 2–12) was prepared by mixing acidic buffer component (0.04 M phosphoric acid, 0.04 M boric acid, and 0.04 M acetic acid) with basic buffer component (0.2 M sodium hydroxide), and was adjusted to the required pH using 0.2 M NaOH (the initial pH of BR solution was 1.81). The NSCDs were dispersed in pH 7.4 BR buffer solution at a concentration of 0.15 mg/mL, and the dispersion was then stored at 4 °C for further experiments.

1.0 g of citric acid and 0.5 g of dithiooxamide were mixed with 7 mL of distilled water. The mixture was then heated using an 800 W domestic microwave oven for 2.5 min and a brownish black solid was achieved. 200 mL distilled water was added to the solid with 10 min stirring. The resultant suspension was filtered to get rid of most insoluble components and then treated with centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 20 min to remove precipitates. The obtained clear liquid was concentrated to 15 mL and dialyzed against DI water using a dialysis membrane (molecular weight cut-off 3.5 kDa) for 2 days to remove ionic impurities. The homogeneous solution containing NSCDs were finally obtained. After evaporation, the concentrated NSCDs were re-dispersed in water to the desired concentrations.

The spectral measurement of UV–Vis absorption was performed on an Agilent 8453 spectrophotometer. FL spectra were measured by a Shimadzu RF-5301 PCFL spectrophotometer. For better understanding and observation, all curves on FL spectra were smoothen through Origin 8 fitting software package and overlap the original data. The XPS spectra of the samples were conducted on a Quantera SXM (ULVAC-PHI, Japan) X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The crystalline structure was recorded by using a D/Max-2500 (Rigaku, Japan) X-ray diffractometer. The scanning transmission electron microscope (STEM) (Titan G2 ChemiSTEM Cs Probe, FEI co., NL) was utilized to characterize the morphology and size of NSCDs. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained with a spectrum GX& AutoImage (PerkinElmer, USA) spectrometer using a KBr pellet as control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of relative FLQY of NSCDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The relative fluorescent quantum yield (FLQY) of NSCDs was calculated by linearizing integrated fluorescence intensities (excitation at 360 nm) of NSCDs and reference (quinine sulfate) versus their corresponding absorbance. The equation of FLQY is:
(1)
Φ X
=
Φ
S T
×
(
G r a
d X
G r a
d
S T
)
×
(
η x 2
η
S T
2
)
where Grad is the slope of the plot, η is the refractive index of the solvent, Φ is the FLQY, X stands for NSCDs, ST refers to quinine sulfate. Quinine sulfate was dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 (refractive index: 1.33) and the C-dots were dissolved in water (refractive index: 1.33). The FLQY of quinine sulfate is known as 0.54 [37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Manufacture of NSCDs-based test paper and its application in real sample detection
ce:section.ce:para: The test paper was prepared by immersing filter paper in NSCDs deionized water solution (15 μg/mL) for 10 min and then dried in oven at 60 °C for 1 h. The water sample was collected from Nakdong River (Daegu, Korea) and was filtered by a 0.45 μm Millipore membrane prior to the use. 100 mL of the filtered river water was spiked with 0.0031 g (1×10−5 mol) of mercuric(II) perchlorate for further analysis.

The morphology of as-synthesized NSCDs was imaged by STEM (see the Supporting Information (SI), Fig. S1). The scattered spherical dots were easily observed, confirming the morphology of carbon dots. The size distribution of NSCDs was analyzed between 1 nm and 4 nm with the average particle size of 2 nm (see the SI, Fig. S1, insert).

The valent state of NSCDs was investigated by XPS technique. The C 1s spectra, as shown in Fig. 1 , reveal the existence of different carbon bonds, wherein the large amount of sp2 and sp3 C–C (284.1 eV) suggests the graphene-like inner core of NSCDs. And the C–N/C–S (284.9 eV and 285.85 eV) demonstrate that NSCDs are N, S- doped. Two sub-bands can be fitted in N 1s spectra (Fig. 1B) and assigned to C–NC and C–N groups, respectively. From O 1s spectra (Fig. 1C), the three peaks at 531.2 eV, 532.1 eV and 533.0 eV are ascribed to CO, C–O–H and OC–O groups, respectively. The S 2p spectra imply the presence of C–S–C units (Fig. 1D, S 2p 1/2 162.75 eV; S 2p 3/2 163.8 eV) [30,38–40]. These results elucidate that the as-synthesized NSCDs contain multiple oxygenated, nitrous, and sulfuric groups.

FTIR and XRD have also been performed to analyze valent states of internal/external structure of NSCDs. In FTIR spectra (see the SI, Fig. S2), the peaks at about 1650 cm−1 and 3192 cm−1 are attributed to the bending and stretching vibration of N–H, respectively. The peak represented stretching vibration of C–N appears at 1399 cm−1. The peak at 2622 cm−1 is suggested as stretching vibration of CS. These results further prove that NSCDs are –N, –S doped carbon dots. The peaks at 812 and 2935 cm−1 are inferred to the stretching and out-of-plane bending of C–H, respectively. Peaks located at 3401 cm−1, 1703 cm−1 and 1176 cm−1 are verified as stretching vibrations of –OH, CO and C–O, respectively, indicating that the as-prepared NSCDs are rich in–OH/-COOH groups. Furthermore, the structural features of NSCDs have also be analyzed by XRD (see the SI, Fig. S3). Comparing to interlayer spacing in graphite (3.4 Å), the larger D value in NSCDs (4.6 Å) indicates that the (002) lattice spacing of carbon-based materials has turbostratic structures [41].

Similar to other carbon dots reported previously [27,42–44], NSCDs have also been investigated possessing excitation-dependent emission, as shown in Fig. 2 A. When exposing NSCDs to a series of excitation wavelength ranging from 300 to 420 nm with an increment of 10 nm, the fluorescent intensity maximum firstly increases and then decreases accompanied with a gradual red-shift (Fig. 2B). Such excitation-dependent mission property has been reported as an important criteria for CDs and is suggested as the synergistic effect of inhomogenous carbogenic core emission and surface state emission [45].

The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of NSCDs have been investigated as well (Fig. 2C). Three absorbance bands centered at around 215, 287 and 347 nm can be marked off on the absorption spectrum. According to some literature, the peak at 215 nm is suggested as the π–π* transition of CC at core [30,46]. The other two absorption peaks at 287 and 347 nm are attributed to the energy trap derived from the heteroatom-passivated surface state [36,47]. This result well matches the assumption that the strong fluorescence of NSCDs is consisted of two emission centers, namely the core state emission and the surface traps emission [30,47,48]. In addition, the excitation and emission bands are also depicted in Fig. 2C (red and blue curves), showing high symmetry.

Recent studies suggested that doping heteroatoms may substantially improve the sensing properties and FLQY of CDs [49]. On the one hand, among many dopants, including nitrogen, sulfur, boron, phosphorous and silicon, N-dopants provide the most effective enhancement to FLQY. On the other hand, S-dopants have also been widely used since they act as versatile ligands for various metal ions. Thus, in order to design capable fluorescent probes which possess both unique sensing properties and high FLQY, the idea of collaborating the advantages of N-dopants and S-dopants within one precursor is quite alluring. Li and Yu et al. described l-cystine-based, N-, S- co-doped CDs which provide high FLQY up to 73% [30]. However, the ambiguous purification (maybe without dialysis, not mentioned) substantially limits their applications as chemosensor. Other currently reported N-, S- co-doped CDs mainly suffer from relatively low FLQY, partially limiting their sensitivities in practical application as well. Comparatively, NSCDs obtained in this work exhibit relatively high FLQY (Φ ≈ 17.6%, see the SI, Fig. S4) even after dialysis (see Table 1). In addition, we have surveyed the doping degree of nitrogen and sulfur in these N-, S- co-doped CDs and tried to find out the underlying rules between FLQY and N/S ratio. Unfortunately, no obvious clue could be gotten. What should be pointed out is that the dialysis (removal of impurities) may strongly influence the measurement of FLQY. The FLQY of NSCDs, for example, decreases after dialysis. Although the fluorescent mechanism of CDs is still an open question, it has been suggested that the FLQY of a certain CDs is more likely affected by surface traps rather than the band-gap modification of inner carbogenic core [50]. Therefore, we suppose that the relatively high FLQY of NSCDs in our work arises from introduction of –CS to surface rather than –SH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multi-sensing for Hg2+, CN− and HS− ions in aqueous condition
ce:section.ce:para: The extreme toxicity, wide distribution and high industrial value make mercury never be away from our sight. According to research data, approximate 4700 tons of mercury are released to environment by human-related activity each year [51,52]. Although 80% of such anthropogenic mercury is released to atmosphere, 15% to lithosphere and 5% to hydrosphere, the mercury emission primarily affects water and aquatic organisms due to the hydrologic cycle and accumulation [52]. Thus, the detection of Hg2+ in aqueous circumstance has gained continuous attention in last few decades [53]. Besides Hg2+, monitoring the existence and the level of cyanide ion is also important because of fatal cytotoxicity versus extensive usage of cyanide in the modern industry [54]. Hydrosulfide anion, a critical ion to the origin of life on earth, remains key functions in some biological processes, such as anaerobic bacterial growth and mammalian physiology. The determination of HS− is still a hot topic in many areas [55]. Thus, to design a versatile sensing platform for rapid and distinctive detection of Hg2+, CN− and HS− in aqueous condition, is an attractive challenge. The Hg2+-affinitive NSCDs make a preliminary attempt.

Nitrogen is an appealing element in the design of chemosensor for detection of ions due to its high activity and peculiar affinity. N-doped CDs have been reported as unique fluorescent probe for detection of various metal ions, such as silver, copper, iron, chromium, mercury, zinc and so on (see the SI, Table S1). In contrast, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs have less been reported. According to some literature, the well-defined coordinating behaviors of RA act differently when binding with different metal cations. For instance, binding with nickel and copper, each RA molecule commonly provides two binding sites for one complex unit, either in the form of –[NH2–M–NH2]– or –[(S)2–M–(NH)2]– [33–35]. But in the case of mercury(II), because of the stronger class B character of mercury, the coordination is in the form of –[S–Hg2+–S]–, wherein each RA molecule provides only one site for one complex unite [33]. During the formation of NSCDs, the none-carbonized CA-RA oligomers may tangle onto the carbon core. The as-synthesized NSCDs, therefore, remain the stronger affinity to Hg2+ than to other metal ions. Meanwhile, NSCDs have also been determined with higher QY than that of some other CDs-based fluorescent probes (see SI, Table S2), further providing their sensitivity in practical application.

To confirm the selectivity of NSCDs to different metal ions, 14 metal cations were applied in the experiment, including Hg2+, Al3+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Ag+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Na+, and K+ (Fig. 3 A and B). Upon the addition of each kind of cations with a concentration of 20 μM to NSCDs in pH 7.4 BR buffer solution, the FL of NSCDs is dramatically quenched by the addition of Hg2+. A weak quenching effect is also observed when adding Cu2+ and Ni2+. This may ascribe to the weak affinity of NSCDs to Cu2+/Ni2+ ions. Apparently, this effect is much weaker than that caused by Hg2. The stronger sensitivity to Hg2+ than that of Cu2+/Ni2+ can also be verified from absorption spectra (see the SI, Fig. S5). The spectrum of CD-Hg complexes shows new absorption band sharply at 280 nm. Reversibly, the absorbance of other two complexes slightly decreases, indicating the totally different binding forms. The quenching mechanism of CD-Hg could be tentatively explained as the non-radiative relaxation via intersystem transition. Meanwhile, competition reaction may take place between original and mercury-coordinated NSCDs in the process of absorbing exciting wavelength, which is the so-called inner filter effect [29,55]. This effect strongly enhances the quenching effect by further cutting off the excitation. It could be experimentally verified from the neogenic absorption band of CD-Hg centered at 280 nm on spectra (see the SI, Fig. S5).

FL titration experiment of NSCDs (15 μg/mL) has also been carried out in pH 7.4 BR buffered solution by adding different amounts of mercury ions. As shown in Fig. 3C, the initial FL intensity of NSCDs gradually decreases as the concentration of Hg2+ increases. The dilogarithm fitting curve (insert) illustrates the variation of FL versus the concentration of mercury(II) in the range of 0–20 μM. The correlation coefficient R2 is 0.9975, indicating the good linearity. The Fig. 3D shows photos of NSCDs water solutions before and after the addition of mercury(II). It is clear that the FL is almost quenched after adding 20 μM Hg2+. The limit of detection for Hg2+ is calculated as 0.18 μM based on the linearity of insert in Fig. 3C (the signal/noise ratio is 150:1 for Shimadzu RF-5301 PCFL spectrophotometer).

The FL that quenched by ion binding may be reversibly restored by adding more competitive counterpart ion. In this work, we find that the FL of NSCDs can substantially regain by adding cyanide anion. To determine the FL recovery ability of different anions for quenched CD-Hg, a variety of common anions have been tested in pH 7.4 BR buffer solution (15 μg/mL of NSCDs with 20 μM of Hg2+). Upon the addition of 40 μM of each anions, including CN−, Cl−, F−, NO3 −, H2PO4 −, AcO−, HSO4 −, HS−, the addition of cyanide ion brings a remarkable recovery to FL intensity, as shown in Fig. 4 A. The FL intensity of quenched CD-Hg is proportionally restored by gradually adding CN− ion (Fig. 4B). The equilibrium is observed on spectrum when the concentration of CN− reaches two folds of Hg2+ (40 μM). And the insert well illustrates the liner relationship between FL intensities at 448 nm and the concentrations of CN−. It is worth noting that the recovered emission band shows slight red-shift comparing to the original one. The raise of such red-shift may stem from the ionized conformation of surface groups (maybe –C–S−) after the disassociation of CD-Hg.

Besides CN−, a weak recovery of FL intensity is also observed by adding hydrosulfide anions (see the SI, Fig. S6). Although this recovery is relatively weak, a color change simultaneously takes place (Fig. 5 A). From the absorption spectra, the absorbance of hydrosulfide evidently increases in ultraviolet and visible region. This may possibly be derived from the formation of non-transparent HgxSy suspensions. The photo evidently shows that mixture solution turns to yellow-brownish after the addition of HS−. (Fig. 5A insert). However, the addition of other anions, including CN−, Cl−, F−, NO3– H2PO4 −, AcO−, HSO4 −, only leads to negligible changes on absorbance spectra (Fig. 5B). Thus, by integrating the absorption and emission spectra, a dual sensing system for distinctive detection of cyanide and hydrosulfide can be established (Fig. 5C).

Working capability of chemosensors is affected by many factors, wherein the pH is an important one. In order to investigate the impact of the pH to FL on-off-on system (namely NSCDs, CD-Hg and CD-Hg + CN−), emission spectra of each state are thoroughly recorded at different pH by using BR buffer solution which can provide a broad effective buffering range.

From emission spectra, the FL intensity of original NSCDs (15 μg/mL) increases along with increasing pH (see the SI, Fig. S7). By adding Hg2+ (20 μM) to NSCDs (15 μg/mL) at different pH, FL intensity shows steadily decrease below pH 8 and is strongly interfered above pH 8 (see the SI, Fig. S8). In the case of FL turn-on (adding CN− to CD–Hg), FL recovery is much enhanced blow pH 4, but too weak to be satisfied within the range of pH 4 to 5 and pH 9–11 (see the SI, Fig. S9). The conclusive illustration is pictured in Fig. 6 . To sum up, in order to effectively apply FL “on-off-on” sensing system, a brightly instinct emission, a considerable quenching degree and an acceptable recovery of intensity should be simultaneously satisfied. The frail emission below pH 6 and invalid quenching ability above pH 8 draw the outline of reasonable working block for NSCDs “on-off-on” sensing system as in the range of pH 6-8 section. This character makes NSCDs be potentially capable of the applications in bio-sensing and imaging fields.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rapid detection of Hg2+ in real water
ce:section.ce:para: Application in real circumstance is always challenging. Thus, we have used NSCDs to design a fancy test paper for rapidly sensing mercury(II) independently without any spectrometers or other large equipment. The filter paper which can avoid high cost and instinct interferences from auto-fluorescing, is chosen as the substrate material.

The test paper is fabricated following the method described in experimental section. It displays a bright blue emission when exposed to UV light (Fig. 7 III). After immersing test paper in Hg2+-spiked river-water (10 mL, Hg2+ 10 μM) without buffering for 1 s, the emission evidently turns to dim (Fig. 7IV). For further development, we are expecting to produce a portable device including miniaturized UV lamp and camera for rapid, inexpensive and quantitive detection of Hg2+ based on our previous work on sensing array consisting of a single indicator [56], in real-time aqueous circumstance.

Highly fluorescent N, S-doped carbon dots (NSCDs) have been fabricated by one step microwave-assisted hydrothermal method. The characterization of NSCDs is thoroughly investigated by using HRTEM, XPS, XRD and FT-IR analysis. The average size of as-obtained NSCDs is 2 nm. NSCDs exhibit bright cyan-bluish excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence. The FLQY of NSCDs is calculated as high as 17.6%. The multi-sensing properties of as-obtained NSCDs have been demonstrated and imply that the NSCDs can be directly used for detection of mercury(II), cyanide and hydrosulfide ions in aqueous condition based on fluorescence “on-off-on” and color changing. By adding Hg2+, notable FL quenching takes place. The quenching mechanism has been inferred as non-radiative relaxation of NSCDs@Hg complexes as well as the inter filter effect which is originated from the competitive absorption of excitation between NSCDs@Hg complexes with the free NSCDs. Further, dual sensing ability for distinctive detection of CN− and HS− has been evaluated. The efficient working block of NSCDs at different pH has been confirm from pH 6 to 8, suggesting potential applications in bio-sensing and imaging areas.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported through the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21177125, 21321064) and the National Research Foundation of Korea grant (Grant No. NRF-2014R1A2A2A01002985) funded by the Korea government (MSIP). The work was also supported by the 100 talents program, which is funded by Chinese Academy of Sciences.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2015.07.051.
